description: A claim taken as a starting point within an argument, so that another claim can be drawn from it. The word names a role, not a truth: a premise can be true, false, or merely hypothetical, and the same proposition can be a premise in one argument and the conclusion of another.
sound_symbolism: 'Mo' begins behind closed lips and opens into a rounded chamber that gives the word a quiet starting place. 'Tha' receives the stress in a stream of dental breath and broad 'a'. 'Re' sets the tongue rolling toward what follows. A premise sounds gathered at first, opened for inspection, then sent onward along a line of thought.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun: 'ha mothare shewo nai.' (This premise is true.) Plural premises take 'lo'. In 'lo mothare ha remuma panoru.' (The premises support this conclusion), the role relation is explicit. Distinct from 'thesori' (evidence), which is material offered in support, and from 'thorui' (beginning), which is any starting point.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Naming a premise encourages attention to what has been provisionally taken up rather than treating the whole argument as an indivisible certainty.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Speakers can challenge a starting claim while preserving the rest of an exchange and without implying hostility toward its author.

tags.cognition: a proposition serving as a starting point in inference
tags.dialogue: a claim participants can grant, question, or revise explicitly
